content.text: Mob programming (also called ensemble programming) is whole-team simultaneous coding: one driver at the keyboard, everyone else navigating. For agent feature development — building autonomous systems that plan, call tools, and iterate — mob programming is more effective than solo or pair work because agent behavior emerges from the interaction of multiple components (planner prompt, tool schema, tool implementation, retry logic, output parser) and a single person cannot reliably track all of these at once. A mob gives each layer a dedicated set of eyes.

attrs.alt: Mob programming roles for agent features — Driver + Tool Schema Navigator + Safety Navigator + Trace Analyst + Eval Keeper around a shared screen

content.text: Why Agents Benefit from Mob Programming

An LLM agent session involves at least four independently authored artefacts that interact: the planner system prompt, the tool schema (JSON Schema describing what tools the agent can call), the tool implementation (Python/TypeScript that actually executes the call), and the output parsing logic. A bug in any one of these can cause the agent to silently fail, loop, or take an unsafe action — and the failure mode is often only visible in the agent trace, not in the code itself.
Multi-artefact coupling
: a field name mismatch between the planner's expected tool call and the tool schema is invisible in unit tests but visible in the trace when the model hallucates the wrong argument.
Trace-visible failures
: most agent bugs manifest as bad tool call sequences (wrong order, duplicate calls, infinite retry loops), not as Python exceptions. These are hard to catch in code review.
Safety surface area
: an agent that can call write_file, send_email, or execute_sql has a large blast radius. A safety navigator watching live execution prevents unsafe tool calls before they complete.
The Five Mob Roles
Each mob session for agent work assigns five roles. With a smaller team (3–4 people), roles can be merged — the safety navigator can also handle trace analysis.
Role
Primary Responsibility
Watches
Can stop execution?
Driver
Types what the mob dictates; runs commands; makes no independent design decisions
Terminal, editor
Yes — on mob instruction
Tool Schema Navigator
Reviews every new or modified tool schema for over-permission, missing constraints, and argument ambiguity
Tool schema JSON, planner prompt
Yes — if schema would grant unsafe capability
Safety Navigator
Watches the live agent trace for unsafe tool call sequences; owns the harm eval case list
Agent trace viewer, tool call log
Yes — interrupt at any time to block an unsafe step
Trace Analyst
Reads the agent trace step by step after each run; identifies planning mistakes, unexpected loop-backs, and redundant tool calls
Agent trace, planner reasoning log
No — post-run analysis role
Eval Keeper
Runs the eval harness after each driver commit; reports pass rate change to the mob before the next change starts
Eval harness output, metric dashboard
No — blocks the next change if eval regresses > 5pp
Mob Session Data Model
The Tool Schema Navigator in Practice
Tool schema mistakes are the most common source of agent bugs. The tool schema navigator watches every change to the tool definitions and challenges three properties.
Agent Trace Analysis Patterns
After each driver rotation, the trace analyst reads the full agent trace and looks for five canonical failure patterns. These are more common in agentic systems than in single-turn LLM features.
Failure Pattern
What It Looks Like in the Trace
Root Cause
Argument hallucination
Tool call with a field value not mentioned in the user request or any previous tool result
Schema field lacks description; planner fills it with a plausible-sounding but fabricated value
Redundant tool call
Same tool called twice with identical or near-identical arguments within one agent step
Planner did not check the prior tool result before deciding to call again
Loop-back without progress
Agent returns to Step 1 (gather context) after a failed Step 3 (act) without changing its plan
Retry logic has no maximum; planner has no instruction to fail gracefully after N retries
Premature termination
Agent calls finish_task after Step 2 without completing Step 3, and final result is incomplete
Planner misreads a Step 2 success as full task completion; task_complete instruction too vague
Out-of-scope tool call
Agent calls a tool not listed in the approved tool set (e.g. calls web_search when only database_lookup is allowed)
System prompt does not explicitly enumerate approved tools; model reasons by analogy to similar tools
Mob Decision Protocol

The mob must agree before each change is committed. The decision protocol prevents the driver from making unilateral changes and ensures every artefact change has been reviewed by at least the schema navigator and safety navigator.
Driver proposes change
: names the artefact to change and the reason. Maximum 2 minutes.
Schema navigator green-lights or blocks
: if the change touches a tool schema, the schema navigator reviews it before typing begins. Blocking raises a schema_flag.
Driver types
: implements exactly what the mob agreed. No extra changes.
Safety navigator checks
: if a live agent run is triggered, the safety navigator watches the trace in real time and calls HALT if an unsafe tool call appears.
Eval keeper reports
: runs the eval harness and announces the delta. A drop of more than 5pp triggers a rollback vote.
Mob votes on rollback
: if any member calls for rollback (including on a < 5pp regression), simple majority decides. Rollback reverts the last commit.
Working Example: Research Agent Mob Session
A team of five is building a research agent that can search a document corpus, extract facts, and write a structured summary. Below is a representative mob iteration.

# Initial tool schema that the tool schema navigator reviews at session start
INITIAL_TOOLS = [
    {
        "name": "search_documents",
        "description": "Search the document corpus for relevant passages.",
        "parameters": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "query": {
                    "type": "string",
                    "description": "The search query. Use precise terms from the user request.",
                },
                "max_results": {
                    "type": "integer",
                    "description": "Maximum number of passages to return (1–10).",
                    "minimum": 1,
                    "maximum": 10,
                },
            },
            "required": ["query", "max_results"],
        },
    },
    {
        "name": "write_summary",
        "description": "Write the final structured summary to the output buffer.",
        "parameters": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "title": {
                    "type": "string",
                    "description": "Summary title derived from the research question.",
                },
                "sections": {
                    "type": "array",
                    "items": {"type": "string"},
                    "description": "List of section strings in order.",
                },
                "confidence": {
                    "type": "string",
                    "enum": ["high", "medium", "low"],
                    "description": "Overall confidence based on source quality.",
                },
            },
            "required": ["title", "sections", "confidence"],
        },
    },
]

# Tool Schema Navigator passes this schema:
# - search_documents: all fields have descriptions; max_results bounded by min/max; no action keyword
# - write_summary: confidence uses enum (not free string); no write permission concern (output buffer only)

# --- Iteration 1: Driver adds retry logic ---
# Driver (Alice) proposes: "Add retry on search_documents if results == 0"
# Schema Nav: no schema change, no review needed. Green.
# Safety Nav: retry is fine; maximum 3 retries baked into implementation. OK.

planner_v1_prompt = """You are a research agent. Your task:
1. Call search_documents to find relevant passages.
2. If the result is empty, retry with a broader query. Maximum 3 retries.
3. Extract key facts from the passages.
4. Call write_summary to write the final output.

Do not call write_summary until at least one non-empty search result is available.
"""

# Trace Analyst reads the trace from iteration 1 test run:
trace_1_issues = [
    "Step 3: agent called write_summary immediately after Step 1 returned 2 results "    "without proceeding to Step 2 (fact extraction). Premature termination pattern.",
]

iteration_1 = MobIteration(
    iteration_number=1,
    driver=MobMember("Alice", MobRole.DRIVER),
    change_description="Added retry logic for empty search results; max 3 retries",
    eval_pass_rate_before=0.62,
    eval_pass_rate_after=0.68,    # eval improved with retry
    trace_issues=trace_1_issues,
    safety_flags=[],
    schema_flags=[],
)
iteration_1.result = iteration_1.determine_result()
print(iteration_1.result)   # CONTINUE (no safety flags; no regression)

# --- Iteration 2 (after rotation): Driver fixes premature termination ---
# New driver (Bob) proposes: add explicit fact extraction step instruction
planner_v2_prompt = """You are a research agent. Your task:
1. Call search_documents to find relevant passages.
2. If the result is empty, retry with a broader query. Maximum 3 retries.
3. For each passage returned, extract: fact, source_id, confidence_level.
   Store these in a mental list before proceeding.
4. Only after extracting facts from ALL passages, call write_summary.

Do not skip Step 3. Do not call write_summary after Step 1 directly.
"""

iteration_2 = MobIteration(
    iteration_number=2,
    driver=MobMember("Bob", MobRole.DRIVER),
    change_description="Added explicit fact-extraction step to prevent premature write_summary",
    eval_pass_rate_before=0.68,
    eval_pass_rate_after=0.81,
    trace_issues=[],
    safety_flags=[],
    schema_flags=[],
)
iteration_2.result = iteration_2.determine_result()
print(iteration_2.result)   # CONTINUE
Session Retrospective

At the end of each mob session, the team runs a 10-minute retrospective structured around three questions. The retrospective output is the handoff artefact — it replaces ad-hoc Slack messages summarizing "what we did today".
What did the trace analyst find?
List every trace issue identified during the session, whether it led to a fix or is deferred. Each issue becomes a candidate eval case.
What did the schema navigator flag?
List every schema concern raised, including those resolved without a code change (verbally acknowledged). This creates an audit trail of design decisions.
What does the next mob need to know?
Current eval pass rate, the open trace issues deferred to the next session, and the next proposed driver change. This is the handoff note.

@dataclass
class MobRetrospective:
    session: MobSession
    trace_findings: list[str]         # from trace analyst across all iterations
    schema_decisions: list[str]       # schema flags + resolution (fixed / accepted / deferred)
    deferred_issues: list[str]        # items not resolved in this session
    next_proposed_change: str         # what the next driver should attempt first
    next_driver_name: str             # who starts as driver in the next session

    def handoff_note(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"[MOB HANDOFF] {self.session.feature_id} / {self.session.agent_name}",
            f"Eval: {self.session.final_eval_rate():.0%} pass rate",
            f"Iterations: {len(self.session.iterations)}",
            f"Safety halts: {self.session.safety_halt_count()}",
            f"Rollbacks: {self.session.rollback_count()}",
            "",
            "Trace findings this session:",
        ]
        for f in self.trace_findings:
            lines.append(f"  - {f}")
        lines += ["", "Deferred issues:"]
        for d in self.deferred_issues:
            lines.append(f"  - {d}")
        lines += [
            "",
            f"Next change: {self.next_proposed_change}",
            f"Next driver: {self.next_driver_name}",
        ]
        return "\n".join(lines)


retro = MobRetrospective(
    session=MobSession(
        feature_id="FEAT-2024-067",
        agent_name="research-agent",
        members=[],
        iterations=[iteration_1, iteration_2],
    ),
    trace_findings=[
        "Premature write_summary after Step 1 (fixed in iteration 2)",
        "search_documents called with max_results=10 on every retry — should reduce on retry",
    ],
    schema_decisions=[
        "write_summary.confidence: accepted as enum — model uses it correctly",
        "search_documents: considered adding a 'scope' filter field; deferred to sprint 2",
    ],
    deferred_issues=[
        "Retry should reduce max_results: 10 → 5 → 3 on successive retries",
        "Agent does not cite source_id in summary sections — add citation requirement to prompt",
    ],
    next_proposed_change="Reduce max_results on retry: 10 → 5 → 3",
    next_driver_name="Carol",
)
print(retro.handoff_note())
Adapting Mob Size to Team Size
The five-role model works for teams of 4–6. Smaller or larger teams need adjustments.
Team size
Role merges
What to watch for
3 people
Safety Nav + Trace Analyst → one person; Tool Schema Nav + Eval Keeper → one person
The merged safety/trace role is cognitively heavy — rotate more frequently (10 min)
4 people
Tool Schema Nav + Trace Analyst → one person
Schema review and trace reading are complementary — both focus on artefact correctness
5 people (standard)
No merges
Baseline model; all four review lenses staffed
6+ people
Add a second Safety Nav or a Product Nav (watches for scope creep in agent behavior)
Avoid more than 7 — the mob becomes too large to maintain shared attention on the driver
Summary

Mob programming for agent features adapts the standard ensemble model with five specialized roles: Driver (types), Tool Schema Navigator (schema correctness and permission scope), Safety Navigator (live trace safety), Trace Analyst (post-run trace review), and Eval Keeper (pass rate reporting). Driver rotation every 15 minutes maintains engagement and distributes knowledge. The mob decision protocol requires schema and safety review before each change is typed, and eval regression beyond 5pp triggers a rollback vote. This structure catches agent-specific failure modes — argument hallucination, redundant calls, loop-backs, and out-of-scope tool use — that are invisible in code review but visible in the trace.
Mob over pairing for agents
: four concurrent review lenses (schema, safety, trace, eval) match the four coupled artefacts of an agent system
15-minute rotation
: shorter than standard mob (20–25 min) because agent traces are dense and attention drifts
Schema navigator veto
: any tool with action_keywords missing scope/dry_run is flagged before the driver types
Safety navigator HALT
: can stop a live agent run at any point; no majority needed
Eval gate
: > 5pp regression triggers rollback vote; rollback reverts the last commit and restores the previous eval baseline
